_meta.title: Suffix Structures in Action — Aho-Corasick, LCP

questions.question: In the suffix array doubling algorithm, what is sorted in each round?
options: Suffixes are sorted from scratch by their full string value
Suffixes are sorted by a key of (rank[i], rank[i + 2^k]) using the previous round's ranking
Characters of the string are sorted alphabetically
Suffixes are sorted by their starting position in the text
questions.explanation: Each doubling round sorts suffixes using the composite key (rank[i], rank[i + k]) where rank[] is the ranking from the previous round and k = 2^(round-1). This refines the sort: after ceil(log2(n)) rounds, all suffixes are uniquely ranked and fully sorted.

questions.question: In Kasai's algorithm, why does processing suffixes in text order (not sorted order) still compute the LCP array in O(n)?
options: Because text order is always the same as sorted order for the LCP computation
Because the LCP between consecutive sorted suffixes is always at most 1
Because moving from suffix i to suffix i-1 in text order, the running LCP drops by at most 1, limiting total character comparisons to O(n)
Because Kasai's algorithm uses hashing to compare suffixes in O(1)
questions.explanation: When we move from suffix i to suffix i-1 in text order, the LCP with the previous sorted neighbor is at least h-1 (where h is the running LCP). We only need to compare characters beyond that. Since h increases by at most 1 per step and decreases to 0 at most n times, total work is O(n).

questions.question: What is the total time complexity of Aho-Corasick for searching a text of length n with patterns totaling length m, producing z matches?
options: O(n * m)
O(n * log m)
O(n + m + z)
O(m * log n + z)
questions.explanation: Aho-Corasick runs in O(n + m + z). Trie construction takes O(m), failure-link BFS takes O(m), and the search phase takes O(n + z): each text character advances at most one trie level (possibly after following failure links whose total work is bounded by n), and each of the z matches is reported in O(1).

questions.question: How many distinct substrings does a string of length n have, and how can the LCP array compute this?
options: Always n^2; subtract the maximum LCP value from n^2
n*(n+1)/2 minus the sum of lcp[1..n-1], because each LCP value counts duplicate prefixes between adjacent sorted suffixes
2^n minus the sum of all LCP values
n minus the number of zero entries in the LCP array
questions.explanation: A string of length n has n*(n+1)/2 total substrings. The LCP array tells us how many are shared between adjacent sorted suffixes. Summing lcp[1] through lcp[n-1] counts every duplicate prefix exactly once, so distinct substrings = n*(n+1)/2 - sum(lcp[1..n-1]).

questions.question: What problem does the Aho-Corasick algorithm solve?
options: Finding the longest palindromic substring
Matching multiple patterns simultaneously in a single text traversal
Computing the edit distance between two strings
Compressing a string using suffix trees
questions.explanation: Aho-Corasick builds a trie of all patterns with failure links (like KMP failure function generalized to a trie). It then processes the text once, matching all patterns simultaneously in O(n + m + z) time where n is text length, m is total pattern length, and z is matches.
